questions.question_text: Two unrelated business partners purchase an investment property in Illinois. They do not specify the form of ownership in the deed. How is title presumed to be held?
questions.correct_answer: Tenancy in common with no right of survivorship
distractors: Joint tenancy with right of survivorship
Tenancy by the entirety with creditor protection
Community property with equal shares
questions.explanation: In Illinois, when unrelated parties take title without specifying ownership type, the default presumption is tenancy in common. Each owner holds an undivided interest that passes to their heirs at death, not to the other co-owner.

questions.question_text: A married couple in Illinois holds title as tenants by the entirety. One spouse incurs a personal judgment debt. Can the creditor force a sale of the property?
questions.correct_answer: No, one spouse's creditor cannot force sale of entirety property
distractors: Yes, creditors may force sale of any jointly held property
Yes, but only after a 12-month waiting period
No, unless the debt exceeds the homestead exemption amount
questions.explanation: Tenancy by the entirety in Illinois provides significant creditor protection. A creditor of only one spouse cannot attach or force the sale of property held in this form. Both spouses must be liable for a creditor to reach the property.

questions.question_text: Four friends want to create a joint tenancy in Illinois. Three contribute equally, but one contributes nothing. They all receive title at the same time via one deed. Is joint tenancy created?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if all four unities of TTIP are satisfied in the deed
distractors: No, because contributions must be equal for joint tenancy
No, joint tenancy requires a blood or marriage relationship
Yes, but only if they file a separate survivorship affidavit
questions.explanation: Joint tenancy requires the four unities of Time, Title, Interest, and Possession (TTIP). Equal financial contribution is not one of the requirements. If all owners receive equal interests from the same deed at the same time with equal possession rights, joint tenancy is valid.

questions.question_text: A seller conveys property using a quitclaim deed in Illinois. Two years later, a title defect from a prior owner is discovered. What warranty protection does the buyer have from this deed?
questions.correct_answer: None, a quitclaim deed provides no warranties of title
distractors: Full protection against all title defects past and present
Protection only for defects arising during the seller's ownership
Protection if the buyer purchased title insurance at closing
questions.explanation: A quitclaim deed transfers only whatever interest the grantor may have, with no warranties of any kind. The buyer has no recourse against the seller for title defects. A warranty deed or special warranty deed would have provided such protections.

questions.question_text: An Illinois deed is properly signed and notarized, then hand-delivered to the grantee. The grantee never records it. Is the transfer valid?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, recording is not required for a valid transfer
distractors: No, Illinois law requires recording to complete the transfer
No, the deed must be recorded within 30 days of delivery
Yes, but only if the grantee files a transfer declaration
questions.explanation: In Illinois, a deed is effective upon proper execution and delivery. Recording is not required for the transfer to be valid between the parties. However, recording protects the grantee against subsequent purchasers who might claim an interest in the property.

questions.question_text: A Chicago investor places property into an Illinois land trust with a bank as trustee. Who holds legal title to the property?
questions.correct_answer: The corporate trustee holds legal title
distractors: The investor retains legal title as beneficiary
Title is split equally between the trustee and beneficiary
The county recorder holds title until the trust terminates
questions.explanation: In an Illinois land trust, the corporate trustee (typically a bank or trust company) holds legal title to the property. The beneficiary retains all management rights and control, but legal title is vested in the trustee's name.

questions.question_text: A beneficiary of an Illinois land trust wants to sell their interest without recording a new deed. Is this possible?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, beneficial interest transfers without a deed
distractors: No, all transfers require a recorded deed
No, the trustee must reconvey title first
Yes, but only after one year in the trust
questions.explanation: One of the key advantages of an Illinois land trust is that the beneficial interest is classified as personal property. It can be transferred by assigning the beneficial interest without recording a new deed, simplifying the transfer process and potentially reducing transfer tax exposure.

questions.question_text: A title searcher checks the public records for an Illinois land trust property. What information about the beneficiary will they find?
questions.correct_answer: Nothing, the beneficiary's identity is not in public records
distractors: The beneficiary's name and ownership percentage
Only the beneficiary's name but not their address
The beneficiary's name if the trust was created after 2010
questions.explanation: Privacy is a primary benefit of the Illinois land trust. Because legal title is held in the trustee's name, public records show only the trustee. The beneficiary's identity remains private, which is a major reason land trusts are popular in Chicago real estate.

questions.question_text: A trustee of an Illinois land trust receives an offer to purchase the trust property. The trustee believes the price is excellent and wants to accept. Can the trustee proceed independently?
questions.correct_answer: No, the trustee acts only at the beneficiary's direction
distractors: Yes, the trustee has full authority over legal title decisions
Yes, if the offer exceeds the property's assessed value
No, unless the trust agreement grants automatic sale authority
questions.explanation: In an Illinois land trust, the trustee holds legal title but has no independent decision-making authority regarding the property. The trustee acts only at the direction of the beneficiary, who retains all management and control rights over the property.

questions.question_text: An investor holds beneficial interest in an Illinois land trust. For legal classification purposes, the beneficial interest is treated as:
distractors: Real property because it relates to real estate
A hybrid of real and personal property
Real property for tax purposes and personal for all others
questions.explanation: A distinguishing feature of the Illinois land trust is that the beneficiary's interest is classified as personal property rather than real property. This classification is what allows transfer by assignment instead of deed and contributes to the privacy and flexibility benefits of the land trust structure.

questions.question_text: An Illinois homeowner has $40,000 in equity in their primary residence. A creditor with a $30,000 judgment attempts to force a sale. How much equity is protected by the homestead exemption?
questions.correct_answer: $15,000 is protected from the judgment creditor
distractors: The full $40,000 because the home is owner-occupied
$25,000 under the revised Illinois homestead statute
$30,000 matching the amount of the judgment
questions.explanation: The Illinois homestead exemption protects $15,000 of a homeowner's equity from forced sale by judgment creditors. The remaining $25,000 in equity above the exemption is potentially available to satisfy the creditor's claim.

questions.question_text: A bank holds a mortgage on an Illinois homeowner's primary residence. The homeowner falls behind on payments. Can the homestead exemption prevent the bank from foreclosing?
questions.correct_answer: No, the homestead exemption does not protect against mortgage liens
distractors: Yes, the $15,000 exemption applies to all creditors equally
Yes, but only if the homeowner has children under 18
No, unless the homeowner files a homestead declaration first
questions.explanation: The Illinois homestead exemption does not apply to voluntary liens such as mortgages. It also does not protect against mechanic's liens or property tax liens. The exemption shields equity only from general judgment creditors seeking forced sale.

questions.question_text: An Illinois resident dies without a will, survived by a spouse and two adult children. How is the estate distributed under intestacy law?
questions.correct_answer: Spouse receives one-half; children split the other half equally
distractors: Spouse receives one-third; children split two-thirds equally
Spouse receives the entire estate regardless of children
Children receive equal shares; spouse gets a life estate only
questions.explanation: Under Illinois intestacy law, when a decedent is survived by both a spouse and descendants, the surviving spouse inherits one-half of the estate. The remaining one-half is divided equally among the descendants.

questions.question_text: An Illinois resident dies intestate with no surviving descendants but a surviving spouse. Under intestacy, who inherits the estate?
questions.correct_answer: The surviving spouse inherits the entire estate
distractors: The estate is split between the spouse and the decedent's parents
The spouse receives half and the state receives the remainder
The estate goes to the closest living blood relative only
questions.explanation: Under Illinois intestacy law, if the decedent leaves a surviving spouse but no descendants, the surviving spouse inherits the entire estate. Parents and siblings only inherit if there is no surviving spouse or if there are specific statutory provisions that apply.

questions.question_text: An Illinois property owner rents out their home and moves to a different city. A judgment creditor later seeks to force a sale. Can the owner claim the homestead exemption?
questions.correct_answer: No, the property must be owner-occupied to qualify
distractors: Yes, once homestead attaches it remains until the property is sold
Yes, if the owner intends to return within one year
No, but the tenant can claim the exemption on the owner's behalf
questions.explanation: The Illinois homestead exemption requires the property to be the owner's primary residence. When the owner moves out and rents the property, it is no longer owner-occupied and the homestead protection is lost. The exemption does not follow the owner to a new residence automatically.
